title: Drones Hit Amazon's Computer Buildings in UAE and Bahrain
summary: Amazon's cloud computing business, called AWS, says drones damaged three of its buildings in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Bahrain. This happened after the US and Israel attacked Iran over the weekend. On Sunday, objects hit a data center in the UAE, causing sparks and fire. In Bahrain, a drone strike near a facility caused power and connection problems. Amazon confirmed on Monday that drone strikes caused these outages. The drones broke parts of the buildings, stopped power, and even caused water damage from firefighting. Amazon is working to fix the damage and restore services, but it might take time. They advise customers in the region to back up their data and move their work to other AWS facilities around the world. This shows how important technology buildings can be at risk during wars.

background_read: This article is about how drones, which are like small remote-controlled planes, attacked buildings owned by Amazon's cloud business in the Middle East. Cloud computing means using big computer centers to store data and run programs online. The attacks happened during a conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran, showing that wars can affect technology we use every day. Amazon is trying to fix the damage and help customers keep their information safe.
Article_Structure: WHAT: Drones damaged three facilities in UAE and Bahrain, causing outages and damage
WHERE: United Arab Emirates and Bahrain
WHY: This happened after US and Israeli strikes against Iran, highlighting risks to technology during conflicts

perspectives.description: The article shows how data centers can be vulnerable during military actions, affecting global services.
